questions.question: A faculty member builds slides by opening PowerPoint, selecting a blank slide, and then placing text boxes, images, and shapes anywhere on the slide. What is the main accessibility concern with this approach?

options.text: The reading order is unpredictable — screen readers may read elements in random order rather than the logical flow the instructor intended
Blank slides are more difficult for students to print out
Images on blank slides cannot have alt text added to them
Using blank slides requires a paid PowerPoint license

questions.explanation: Built-in slide layouts have a predefined structure and reading order. When you use a blank slide and place elements manually, PowerPoint reads them in creation order — not in the order you see them on screen. For example, a title placed last on the slide might be announced first by a screen reader. Using built-in layouts ensures correct reading order and semantic structure.

questions.question: A faculty member's presentation includes many photographs and diagrams that are critical to understanding the content. Why might this presentation need a companion document for accessibility?

options.text: Because screen reader users cannot see images, and a companion document can provide detailed descriptions that make the visual content equivalent in text form
Because companion documents are required by WCAG 2.2 for all presentations
Because PowerPoint slides cannot include alt text
Because students with disabilities are not allowed to attend live presentations

questions.explanation: While alt text helps, a companion document provides fuller descriptions of complex visual content. For highly visual presentations (art history, architecture, design, science labs with demonstrations), a detailed companion — describing what happens in each image, how visual elements relate, and what students are meant to learn — ensures that students using screen readers or not attending live can access equivalent content.
